paragraphs: I don't know about you, but that cracked my jar.[#pause] So, you'll notice a change here. Well, Frank did at least.[#pause] Don't worry—nothing has gone wrong.[#pause] I think.

We're stretching ideas. Making room. Thinking bigger.[#pause] That requires a bit of tidying up.[#pause] This is progress. Perhaps not in the right direction.[#pause] That's the problem in zero-g sometimes—having a sense of direction.
So, what I'm trying to say is, please adjust your expectations accordingly.

sections.heading:
16 CLICKS POST UNFOLDING 
ORBITAL THREE "THE EARS"
paragraphs: Frank lived in a tangle of fiber-optic cables. Wires snaked out from diagnostic panels and powered the custom holo-projector. [#holopong]It spun a colorful display of fractal light. The bulkheads were covered with scribbled notes from signal analyses. Between them, the photos told a different story. One showed his late husband, George, smiling so brightly it was painful. The picture had been taken a year before he'd died. Another showed their adopted son, Mo, looking proud in his new FNS *Alsvin* uniform. One photo showed him, still young and hopeful, with his parents on Sol Academy graduation day. Another was a silly shot of him and his Academy friends giving a thumbs-up in a cramped shuttle. The last showed his mission colleagues, now spread out at different listening posts.

paragraphs.text: "[soft]Query," Gary said softly. "[soft]Heart rate has increased by twelve percent.[#pause] Cortisol levels are rising. Is this a negative stimulus?"
paragraphs.speaker: gary

paragraphs.text: "[sad]No, Gary," Frank said with a sad smile. "[sad]It's a memory."
paragraphs.speaker: frank-muro

paragraphs: He hadn't reset Gary. Even with the hallucinations, he couldn't bring himself to do it. Resetting the CIX would mean losing another friend all over again.
[#hum-throb]The hum returned. It began as a vibration in his jaw.

paragraphs.text: "[muttering]That damned hum's rampin' up," Frank muttered, pushing away from the bulkhead. "[focused]I can even feel it. Gary, run a full-spectrum diagnostic. Pinpoint the source of that resonance."
paragraphs.speaker: frank-muro

paragraphs.text: "[#static][glitching]IT'S ### NOISY IN HERE!" the CIX chirped, briefly garbled by static.
paragraphs.speaker: gary

paragraphs: Frank squeezed the valve of his drink pouch and froze halfway through the motion. A drop of water escaped and hung floating in front of his nose. The bead vibrated into geometric shapes.

paragraphs.text: *Cymatics*, Frank realized.
"[skeptical]It shouldnae be doin' that at all," Frank said. "[skeptical]The conduits are shielded."
paragraphs.speaker: frank-muro

paragraphs: He glided to his main console.

paragraphs.text: "[focused]It's creepin' in somewhere. Gary, isolate it and check for any hidden waveforms. Use the power grid as a carrier wave."
paragraphs.speaker: frank-muro

paragraphs.text: "[robotic]ISOLATING…"
paragraphs.speaker: gary

paragraphs: [#holopong]The holo-projector flickered, dissolving into a simple, smooth sine wave showing the station's power cycle. A second waveform appeared on top of it.

paragraphs.text: "[intrigued]That's no' digital." Frank leaned closer. "[intrigued]It looks biological."
paragraphs.speaker: frank-muro

paragraphs.text: "[analytical]Cross-referencing signal architecture with known phenomena," Gary stated.
paragraphs.speaker: gary

paragraphs: A progress bar filled with agonizing slowness.

paragraphs.text: "[robotic]HIGH CORRELATION DETECTED.[#pause] Match with the bio-electric field patterns recorded from Mairee's Myco-Rhizomic System."
paragraphs.speaker: gary

paragraphs: Frank stared, thoughts racing. The planet's living soil was affecting the station's power grid, turning it into a huge antenna.

paragraphs.text: "[wonder]But it's no' just racket either." He traced the looping patterns on the hologram. "[wonder]It's structured. Look at the clusters repeatin'!"
paragraphs.speaker: frank-muro

paragraphs.text: "[cheerful]ANALYZING," Gary said, its voice back to its cheerful affectation. "[cheerful]The waveform is analogous to melodic syntax."
paragraphs.speaker: gary

paragraphs.text: Frank could hardly believe it. "[astonished]So, she's singin' at us!"
paragraphs.speaker: frank-muro

paragraphs: [#chime]A priority notification chimed. Orbital Command.

paragraphs.text: "[dismissive]No' that nyaff again."
paragraphs.speaker: frank-muro

paragraphs: He [#tapping]tapped the console, heart pounding, and accepted the connection. Commander Bellamy's stern face materialized.

paragraphs.text: "[#holopong][stern]Lieutenant Muro," she began. "[cold]Your bio-telemetry is worrying. Cortisol up. REM sleep down for eight clicks.[#pause] You're running on fumes, Lieutenant."
paragraphs.speaker: eva-bellamy

paragraphs.text: "[evasive]Aye, it's…[#pause] the climate controls," Frank said, twitching. "[evasive]I'm stable."
paragraphs.speaker: frank-muro

paragraphs.text: "[cold]I'm reviewing your logs," Bellamy continued. "[cold]There are…[#pause] irregularities. Unscheduled system queries. Accessing restricted manifests. Alterations to comms metadata.[#pause] Poetry…[#pause] in comment lines." She sneered.
paragraphs.speaker: eva-bellamy

paragraphs.text: "[stammering]Syntactic decay," Frank stammered. "[stammering]Happens."
paragraphs.speaker: frank-muro

paragraphs.text: "[flat]Your CIX disagrees," Bellamy said.
paragraphs.speaker: eva-bellamy

paragraphs.text: "[shocked]What? Gary?![#pause] Gary wouldnae…[#pause] He—"
paragraphs.speaker: frank-muro

paragraphs.text: "[cold]It reports you're having conversations with power conduits," she said.
paragraphs.speaker: eva-bellamy

paragraphs: Ice flooded Frank's veins. Gary. The CIX had betrayed him.

paragraphs.text: "[panicked]That's a glitch!" Frank's voice cracked, rising in pitch. "[panicked]The CIX is hallucinatin'! It's way past its reset cycle! It's the one that's unstable, no' me! It's been spoutin' poetry for clicks! See?"
paragraphs.speaker: frank-muro

paragraphs: Bellamy's expression didn't change, but a profound, almost pitying weariness entered her eyes.

paragraphs.text: "[pitying]I see."
paragraphs.speaker: eva-bellamy

paragraphs: Her fingers moved on a console out of his view.

paragraphs.text: "[cold]For your well-being, Lieutenant," she said, voice cold, "[cold]your clearance is revoked.[#pause] A shuttle will come when available. Sober up till then."
paragraphs.speaker: eva-bellamy

paragraphs.text: "[desperate]No! Wait! Don't! I DON'T WANT TO WA—"
paragraphs.speaker: frank-muro

paragraphs: The holoscreen snapped back to swirling geometric art. [#clamps]A heavy thunk and clank echoed through the habitat as the magnetic locks on his quarters engaged. [#handslam]He slammed his fist against the console. The impact, useless in zero gravity, only made him angrier as it sent him spinning.

paragraphs.text: "[enraged]That cold-hearted harpy!"
paragraphs.speaker: frank-muro

paragraphs: Rage turned bitter as he spun to face the main CIX optical sensor.

paragraphs.text: "[accusing]And ye traitor![#pause] Ye tattling, code-rottin' scrapheap!"
paragraphs.speaker: frank-muro

paragraphs: Only now, as the outburst faded and the silence pressed in, did Frank feel the sting of isolation. A soft, synthesized voice came from the main speakers. It wasn't Gary's usual cheerful tone. It wasn't the sad whisper, either. It almost sounded like his own.

paragraphs.text: "[eerie]The upshot o' the downshot is this:[#pause] she won't bother us anymore."
paragraphs.speaker: gary

paragraphs: Frank stopped spinning. The CIX had spoken with his own turn of phrase.

sections.heading: HAB-PERERA

paragraphs.text: "[urgent]It's starting," Joana said.
paragraphs.speaker: joana-perera

paragraphs: She guided Susana to the nest of cushions on the floor.

Ian stood at the main atmospheric vent. He had checked the system many times. The bypass he'd built used a secondary intake tube with a catalytic converter, feeding into a small, sealed CO2 recycling tank hidden behind the water reclamation unit. Spoofed sensors on the main vent would relay normal pressure and airflow readings to the Grid, leading the system to believe the agent had been successfully administered.

paragraphs.text: "[whispered]Here we go," Joana whispered as Susana's eyes grew wide and empty.
paragraphs.speaker: joana-perera

paragraphs: [#gutteral]The seizure struck. The tonic phase began with jolting violence. All the air was forced from Susana's small lungs in a single rush, a sound that sliced through the silence like the cry of a broken bird. Her back arched, her muscles locked rigid. Joana turned Susana onto her side, cushioned her head, and started the chronometer on her slate.

paragraphs.text: "[grounding]You are Susana Perera. You are seven years old.[#pause] The floor is beneath you. My hand is on your arm. You can hear my voice.[#pause] This is my voice. This is real."
paragraphs.speaker: joana-perera

paragraphs: Lines thrown across the dark, something Susana could catch when awareness came back in pieces. Giving her something stable to return to when the storm finally broke.
[#chime]The hab's main Grid interface chimed.
< ALARM: TONIC-CLONIC SEIZURE DETECTED. />

Ian turned to the vent. The bypass light flickered green, then amber. [#airhiss]The main vent hissed, releasing gases into the regulator. None of it escaped into the room. The bypass was working. The agent was being contained.

paragraphs.text: "[steady]One minute," Joana said, her hand a steady pressure on Susana's shoulder.
paragraphs.speaker: joana-perera

paragraphs: The clonic phase began, the rhythmic convulsions less violent than before.

paragraphs.text: "[grounding]Your name is Susana. You are safe. I am here.[#pause] This will pass."
paragraphs.speaker: joana-perera

paragraphs: Planetary Acclimatization Syndrome. PAS, as she had come to call her daughter's condition. The Grid could stop the attack. But in doing so, it also shut down the very part of her brain that was trying to attune itself to this new world. Each suppressed attack slowed the adaptation. The attacks would grow more severe. Left unguided, they could lead to oxygen deprivation. To permanent brain damage. So she chose to break the pattern—to guide her daughter through it, convulsion by convulsion. The die was cast.

Ian stood still, watching Su's little body shake. His own fists trembled. He felt an urge to stop the bypass and let the Grid's solution take over to end her suffering. But he forced himself to stay calm, jaw tight and heart racing.
Joana touched her daughter's hot skin. She listed her clinical observations:

paragraphs.text: "[clinical]Pupils fixed, respiration shallow, cyanosis at the nail beds,"
paragraphs.speaker: joana-perera

paragraphs: followed by a repetitive,

paragraphs.text: "[trembling]You are safe. I am here. This will pass."
paragraphs.speaker: joana-perera

paragraphs: The chronometer ticked past the second pulse marker. Every second beyond it increased the risk of brain damage. Ian broke into sobs, while Joana trembled, clinging to the litany.

paragraphs.text: "[trembling]You are safe.[#pause] I am here."
paragraphs.speaker: joana-perera

paragraphs: But the storm passed. Susana's body relaxed in deep exhaustion after the seizure. Joana brushed the damp hair from her daughter's forehead. After a few moments, Susana's eyes opened. The empty, distant look was gone, replaced by a tired but familiar light.
"Mommy?" she whimpered.

paragraphs.text: Tears ran down Joana's cheek as she held her daughter close. "[sobbing]I'm here." She sobbed with deep relief. "[sobbing]I'm here."
paragraphs.speaker: joana-perera

paragraphs: Ian gasped for air, the tension draining out of him in a long, shuddering breath. He leaned the back of his head against the wall and sank to the floor. He rubbed his chest; his ribs ached as if he himself had endured the convulsions. A moment later, [#chime]the interface chimed again. The Grid, too, believed it had done its job.

< INTERVENTION SUCCESSFUL. 
NEURO STABILITY ACHIEVED. />

After a while, the hab grew quiet, finally letting go of a day that almost refused to end. A rhythmic purring of Joana and Susana filled the small space. Ian lay there for a thud, staring at the shadows of the overhead pipes until they started to look like veins.
Unable to sleep, he quietly put on his jumpsuit and slipped out toward 4-Iota.

Ian peered through the window. The curtains hadn't been drawn, and through the thick glass, Oliver looked like a broadcast. The boy was a ghost bathing in the harsh blue of a schematic projection. His face was a pale, skin drawn tight over his cheekbones, his eyes bloodshot and tracking lines of code with a frantic, mechanical speed. His hands were moving, fingers twitching in the air as if he were trying to grab the data and pull it apart, weaving through an interface only he could see.

[#tap]Ian tapped the window gently, a soft clack of knuckles on reinforced plex. One heartbeat. Two. Five. Oliver's hands dropped to his sides like dead weights. He turned his head and sluggishly raised himself from the chair. [#doorhiss]The door cycled open with a hiss. Ian stepped into the room, which smelled of unwashed clothes and overclocked hardware.

paragraphs.text: "[soft]I thought you'd still be up," Ian said softly.
paragraphs.speaker: ian-perera

paragraphs.text: "[tense]The air scrubbers in this section are cycling at three percent above the standard rate.[#pause] Can you feel it? The pressure?" Oliver said. An unusual welcome.
paragraphs.speaker: oliver-savannen

paragraphs.text: "[observing]I hadn't noticed," Ian said, moving inside. He gave the room a brief once-over before his attention settled on the display. "[observing]What are we looking at?[#pause] Looks like Directorate stuff."
paragraphs.speaker: ian-perera

paragraphs.text: Oliver pointed at the wallscreen. "[bitter]Directorate's daily brief. They're calling the Fold closure a 'persistent systemic anomaly.'" He gave a short, bitter laugh. "[bitter]That's what they call it when the universe wipes your whole past and future from the map.[#pause] And somehow…" He trailed off, his voice cracking.
paragraphs.speaker: oliver-savannen

paragraphs: His movements were jerky, and he started pacing the available floor space. He looked at a framed schematic of the *Alsvin*, and his hands went to his hair, pulling at the roots.

paragraphs.text: "[breaking]I feel it's all my fault! I was so angry at her. At Mom. Over dumb stuff.[#pause] I told her the Alsvin post was a waste of time. That she didn't need—" He choked on the words, a sob catching in his throat.
paragraphs.speaker: oliver-savannen

paragraphs: Oliver's fingers picked the edge of the counter.

paragraphs.text: "[quiet]My dad got us two spots in a Smashout league," he continued quietly. "[quiet]He was proud of it.[#pause] But I bailed. I went to a hackathon instead. Didn't even look up when I told him 'maybe next time.'"
paragraphs.speaker: oliver-savannen

paragraphs: There wasn't a next time.

paragraphs.text: Oliver shook his head. "[haunted]Sometimes I think if I'd just—I don't know.[#pause] Been different. Less difficult. Maybe she wouldn't have left.[#pause] Maybe he wouldn't have—" He exhaled sharply. "[haunted]It keeps running. The thought. Looping. Every night.[#pause] That this isn't really happening.[#pause] That I'm not really here."
paragraphs.speaker: oliver-savannen

paragraphs: The boy's gaze drifted for a second, unfocused.

paragraphs.text: Ian held his shoulder. "[careful]Don't blame yourself, kid. You're too smart for that.[#pause] Your mother will make it through," he said, though the words felt false. The kid deserved the truth. The real one. Another day. Not tonight.
paragraphs.speaker: ian-perera

paragraphs.text: "[doubting]Will she?"
paragraphs.speaker: oliver-savannen

paragraphs: The silence that followed was heavy.

paragraphs.text: "[confessing]My brother is on the Mimir," he said. "[confessing]I know it's not the same."
paragraphs.speaker: ian-perera

paragraphs.text: Oliver's shoulders tensed. "[pressing]Don't you want to know what really happened?"
paragraphs.speaker: oliver-savannen

paragraphs.text: "[careful]To the Fold? I'm sure our best people are working on it."
paragraphs.speaker: ian-perera

paragraphs.text: "[pressing]But how? We're all stuck here. I need to do something. I'm not a kid anymore.[#pause] But the Grid…[#pause] I want to be free of it. Dad had something called the COG Egg. It can access the Grid's network remotely.[#pause] With your clearance, we could get it. It's in the depot."
paragraphs.speaker: oliver-savannen

paragraphs: The terror of the gamble Ian had taken with his daughter's life was still too raw.

paragraphs.text: "[firm]No. Absolutely not. I don't have clearance there.[#pause] Even if I did, I took a risk tonight for my family. I won't do it again." He tried to sound gentler. "[softer]It will be okay. They'll fix this."
paragraphs.speaker: ian-perera

paragraphs.text: "[bitter]You don't believe that. They feed us paste as if we're babies. I'm sick of it." Oliver's frustration matched Ian's own. "[accusing]How can you work on it?!" Oliver accused. "[accusing]The Grid. It's based on my father's system.[#pause] And now you're twisting it into a cage."
paragraphs.speaker: oliver-savannen

paragraphs.text: "[defensive]I don't have a choice!" Ian's words came out raw and defensive. "[defensive]It's my job. We have limited supplies. It's the only way to keep things fair and give everyone a chance before—"
paragraphs.speaker: ian-perera

paragraphs.text: "[firm]But it's wrong."
paragraphs.speaker: oliver-savannen

paragraphs: Ian looked at the boy, seeing his fierce intelligence.

paragraphs.text: "[softer]You're right," Ian said, softer. "[softer]It's wrong. And you have every reason to hate me for it." He paused, letting the unexpected agreement hang in the air. "[earnest]But if you want to be useful, well…" He met Oliver's wary look. "[earnest]They're adding more people to the engineering teams. I can bring on an apprentice.[#pause] You might hate the work. You might even hate me for asking.[#pause] But if you join me, you'll learn about the Grid and more."
paragraphs.speaker: ian-perera

paragraphs.text: "[incredulous]You want me to help you expand the Grid?!" Oliver exclaimed, incredulous.
paragraphs.speaker: oliver-savannen

paragraphs.text: "[direct]No," Ian corrected, cutting through the boy's anger. "[direct]I want you to learn how it's built.[#pause] You can't fix an engine by staring at the casing."
paragraphs.speaker: ian-perera

paragraphs: Oliver stared at Ian for a moment, searching. Then his shoulders relaxed.

paragraphs.text: "[relenting]When do I start, then?"
paragraphs.speaker: oliver-savannen

paragraphs.text: "[direct]Tomorrow. Oh-six.[#pause] I'll pick you up."
paragraphs.speaker: ian-perera
